entry.title: The Atlantic: 12 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • How I Learned to Eat Alone and Not Be Lonely
  • Biden Is Right About Saudi Arabia
  • Make America Remember Again
  • The Most Important Study in the Abortion Debate
  • The One Key Word Biden Needs to Invoke on Ukraine
  • ‘I’m, Like, This Close to Snapping’
  • The Moral Desolation of the GOP
  • What Chesa Boudin Revealed About an Undemocratic Election System
  • Life, Literature, This Moment of June
  • Now Even NASA Wants to Talk About UFOs
  • How Did They Get Inflation So Wrong?
  • An Ode to My Thesaurus
  • The Six Forces That Fuel Friendship
  • A Negative COVID Test Has Never Been So Meaningless
  • She Wouldn’t Exist if Not for Her Friend’s Family
  • White Author, Black Paragons
  • The Other Cause of January 6
  • In Ukraine, Youth Has Ended
  • Photos of the Week: Office Bear, Market Train, Pizza Championship
  • Trump Gets the January 6 Trial He Long Dodged
  • The One Witness at the January 6 Hearing Who Matters Most
  • Monkeypox Could Be Nothing—Or It Could Be the Next Syphilis
  • The Delightful Pretentiousness of Irma Vep
  • Want to Understand Socrates and Sartre? Talk With Your Kid.
  • The One Group That Could Make a Difference on Gun Control
  • American Rasputin
  • How San Francisco Became a Failed City
  • Why Is Dad So Mad?
  • Is This the End of the George Floyd Moment?
  • Are Iowa’s Democratic Days Gone for Good?
  • The Club That No One Wants to Join
  • Sharon McMahon Has No Use for Rage-Baiting
  • Pennsylvania Becomes the Land of Oz
  • No, Ann Coulter, I Am Not Responsible for the ‘Great Replacement’ Theory
  • The Revolt Against Homelessness
  • Why ‘The Watergate Three’ Are Remembered as a Duo
  • The Vindication of Jack White
  • I Just Wanted to Watch People Get Eaten By Dinosaurs
  • The Thin Line Between Sickness and Health
  • Invitation
  • BTS Gets It
  • The Indian Action Blockbuster That Should Make Hollywood Jealous
  • The Ideology of the Bicycle
  • More Than 100 Days of War in Ukraine
  • The United States Must Stand Up for One of Its Own
  • Boris Johnson Has Only Delayed the Inevitable
  • Cheese-Rolling Returns to Cooper’s Hill
  • What the Veneration of Gandhi’s Killer Says About India
  • Are We Doomed?
  • Vaccines for the Littlest Kids Have Already Flopped

  • Do Voters See a Difference Between Crime and Homelessness?
  • America’s Duty to Help the World’s Vulnerable: 13 Reader Views

News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

entry.title: Vox: 12 Jun, 2022

General interest news site

entry.title: Wired Magazine: 12 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The Unwritten Laws of Physics for Black Women
  • Volodymyr Zelensky on War, Technology, and the Future of Ukraine
  • The Multifarious Multiplexity of Taika Waititi
  • Bill Gates Is So Over This Pandemic
  • Paradise at the Crypto Arcade: Inside the Web3 Revolution
  • Life Is Great in the Age of No Secrets
  • Prediction Engines Are Like Karma: You Get What You Stream
  • Six-Word Sci-Fi: Stories Written by You
  • The ‘Form’ Element Created the Modern Web. Was It a Big Mistake?
  • Volodymyr Zelensky and the Art of the War Story
  • Can Social Media Be Redeemed?
  • Fast, Cheap, and Out of Control: Inside Shein’s Sudden Rise
  • Welcome to Janelle Monáe’s Dreamworld
  • Do I Really Need to Back Up All My Data?
  • The Intoxicating Pleasure of Conspiratorial Thinking
  • The End of Alcohol
  • Time Got So Much Weirder. The World Needs a New Lexicon
  • This Startup Wants to Get in Your Ears and Watch Your Brain
  • A Farmer’s Quest to Beat California’s Waves of Drought and Deluge
  • The Bitcoin Bust That Took Down the Web’s Biggest Child Abuse Site
  • A Million Little Pieces: The Race to Rebuild the World’s Reefs
  • If Someone Is Typing, Then Stops … Can I Ask Why?
  • I Finally Reached Computing Nirvana. What Was It All For?
  • The Enduring Legacy of Clubhouse’s Chatty Revolution
  • Is Russia’s Largest Tech Company Too Big to Fail?
  • It’s Like GPT-3, but for Code—Fun, Fast, and Full of Flaws
  • The Infinite Reach of Joel Kaplan, Facebook’s Man in Washington
  • ‘I’m the Operator’: The Aftermath of a Self-Driving Tragedy
  • Trapped in Silicon Valley’s Hidden Caste System
  • Zillow Botched Buying Homes. Opendoor Thinks It Can Do Better
  • The History—and Disturbing Resurrection—of Black Androids
  • Jacques Vallée Still Doesn’t Know What UFOs Are
  • The Best Way to Learn Online? Be a Lurker
  • Driving While Baked? Inside the High-Tech Quest to Find Out
  • Ada Palmer and the Weird Hand of Progress
  • How Telegram Became the Anti-Facebook
  • Do Good Doorbell Cams Make Good Neighbors?
  • 6 Apps That Take the Grind out of Your Workday
  • They Were ‘Calling to Help.’ Then They Stole Thousands
  • North Korea Hacked Him. So He Took Down Its Internet
  • Here Come the Underdogs of the Robot Olympics
  • Is It OK to Listen to a Butt-Dial Message?
  • This 22-Year-Old Builds Chips in His Parents’ Garage
  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
  • The Best of CES 2022
  • In Praise of Unglamorous American Invention
  • Covid Will Become Endemic. The World Must Decide What That Means
  • Advice to Startup Founders: Prepare to Fail
  • The Quest to Trap Carbon in Stone—and Beat Climate Change
  • The Morbid War Over Online Obituaries
  • The Wave-Conquering, Metaverse-Crashing Life of Kai Lenny
  • Welcome to Miami, Where All Your Memes Come True!
  • 4 Dead Infants, a Convicted Mother, and a Genetic Mystery
  • The Twitter Wildfire Watcher Who Tracks California’s Blazes
  • The Matrix Is the Best Hacker Movie
  • Welcome to the (Synthetic) Meatspace
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: Economist: 11 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Politics
  • Business
  • KAL’s cartoon
  • Economic growth: Britain’s real problem
  • The Capitol-riot hearings: High time for prime time
  • Sanctions and Russia: Seizing the moment
  • Xenophobia in South Africa: Scapegoating Africans
  • Short-sightedness: Protecting the pupils
  • Foundation models: Artificial intelligence’s new frontier
  • Letters: On Russia, gun control, graphene, Tokyo, American-British politics, bears
  • AI: Artificial neural networks are making strides towards consciousness, according to Blaise Agüera y Arcas
  • AI: Artificial neural networks today are not conscious, according to Douglas Hofstadter
  • Russia and Ukraine: NATO should avoid learning the wrong lessons from Russia’s blunder in Ukraine, says Michael Kofman
  • Artificial intelligence: The world that Bert built
  • The January 6th hearings: The insurrection televised
  • The Pennsylvania Senate race: Hearts and minds
  • Chesa Boudin: The City by the Bay says nay
  • Gun reform: High noon
  • Foster care: Relative safety
  • Lexington: The incapable state
  • Latin American economies: Ripple effects
  • Canada: A politer kind of populism
  • Bello: The invincible industry
  • Afghanistan’s economy: Guns over butter
  • Industrial disasters: Terminal containers
  • India and the Muslim world: Hands off the Prophet
  • South Korea: Troubled energy
  • Banyan: A little more conversation
  • Foreign policy: A new challenge to the West
  • Being gay in China: Pride and prejudice
  • Consumer spending: Locked down and pent up
  • Chaguan: Hotheads threaten a cold war
  • Xenophobia in South Africa: The death of a pan-Africanist dream
  • Education in Africa: Golden tickets
  • An interview with Israel’s prime minister: Bolder abroad, embattled at home
  • Land reclamation in the Gulf: Cities in the sea
  • French parliamentary elections: Damage control
  • Turkey: Erdogan’s war on everyone
  • Ukraine: Hide and seek
  • Fighting in Donbas: Grinding on
  • Norway and Russia: High noon in the High North
  • Charlemagne: Charge!
  • The economy: Stagnation nation
  • The prime minister: The divine right of Boris
  • Grenfell Tower: After the fire
  • Levelling up: Powerhouse or gateway?
  • Bagehot: The Unconservatives
  • Justice in retreat: Overruled
  • Managing American business in hard times: No margin for error
  • Corporate psychedelics: The ultimate business trip
  • Fast fashion: A pretty picture
  • Big tech in China: Alive. Unwell?
  • Airlines: Flying start
  • Bartleby: Corporate jet, Rorschach test
  • Schumpeter: The Committee to Save the Planet
  • Economic warfare: Seizing opportunities
  • Petrol prices: Squeeze in the middle
  • The euro area: Danger zone
  • Buttonwood: The dash for cash
  • Housing in Canada: Northern frights
  • Interest-rate rises: Treasury seekers
  • Free exchange: A growth industry
  • Ophthalmology: The eyes have it
  • Flood warnings: Earthshaking
  • Music therapy: A drum, a drum, Clem Burke doth come
  • Agricultural entomology: Son, et lumière
  • A fossil feast: Ancient mayflies
  • Architecture and design: Hot property
  • World in a dish: The way of the monkfish
  • Indonesia: In the shadows
  • Plastic surgery: The good doctor
  • Josephine Baker: On the world stage
  • Back Story: Postcard from Hades
  • Indicators: Economic data, commodities and markets
  • Chinese social mobility: Class revival
  • The Economist explains: How Ukraine is fighting back against Russian artillery
  • The Economist explains: Is Kaliningrad, Russia’s exclave surrounded by EU countries, an asset or a liability?
  • Dervla Murphy: Have bike, will travel
Global news and current affairs from a European perspective. Best downloaded on Friday mornings (GMT)

entry.title: Nautilus: 10 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Gravitational Waves Continue to Astound
  • Were It Not for Cosmic Good Fortune, We Wouldn’t Be Here
  • We Were Here
  • Mysteries Are to Be Embraced, But Also to Be Solved
  • A Voice for Minorities in Aquaculture
  • We Better Think Twice About What We Say to ET
  • How Much Is the Ocean Worth?
  • Reshuffled Rivers Bolster the Amazon’s Hyper-Biodiversity
  • Who Are the Scientists Here?
  • The Race to Explore the Ocean’s Twilight Zone
  • The Oceans Are Teeming with Unknown Species
  • The Giraffe Neck Evolved for Sexual Combat
  • Life’s First Peptides May Have Grown on RNA Strands
  • You Eat a Credit Card’s Worth of Plastic Every Week
  • Unlocking Mom’s Brain
  • No Two Human Brains Are Alike
  • “I Have to Admit, I Have a Very Low Opinion of Human Beings”
  • Portrait of the Human as a Young Hominin
  • What Is Time?
  • Doctor Strange and the Multiverse in Science
  • The Secrets of the Blind Salamander
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.title: TIME: June 20th, 2022 | Vol. 199, No. 23 | U.S.

entry.title: Nature: 9 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The sustainability movement is 50. Why are world leaders ignoring it?
  • Science must overcome its racist legacy: Nature’s guest editors speak
  • To boost South Korea’s basic science, look to values, not just budgets
  • A poisonous shield, a potent venom: these worms mean business
  • Who needs sugar? ‘Oleogels’ help the medicine go down
  • Robot surgeons steer smoothly with help from magnet-free motor
  • That quick morning coffee might lead to enduring brain changes
  • The skilled ecosystem engineers with big teeth and paddle tails
  • Vesuvius victim yields first human genome from Pompeii
  • The surprising locales where rivers are prone to jump their banks
  • Metal waste and dodging long COVID
  • The number of researchers with dual US–China affiliations is falling
  • ‘Unsustainable’: how satellite swarms pose a rising threat to astronomy
  • Monkeypox outbreaks: 4 key questions researchers have
  • How the giraffe got its neck: ‘unicorn’ fossil could shed light on puzzle
  • Mathematics prizes have a gender problem — can it be fixed?
  • COVID death tolls: scientists acknowledge errors in WHO estimates
  • Floating solar power could help fight climate change — let’s get it right
  • Conflict’s impact raises costs for Arctic shipping and the climate
  • Climate alone won’t define future worlds
  • Funding: end ‘publish or perish’ for postdocs
  • Complement CVs with curated portfolios
  • The sting of sizeism in the scientific workplace
  • Pico pioneer: building tiny satellites for the Internet of Things
  • How wingless salamanders fly
  • Nanoscale map shows how interfaces impede vibrations
  • Large-scale early urban settlements in Amazonia
  • Blood’s life history traced through genomic scars
  • A chromosome predisposed for sex
  • Diverse data tighten constraints for neutron stars
  • X-ray astronomy comes of age
  • Binarity of a protostar affects the evolution of the disk and planets
  • Constraining neutron-star matter with microscopic and macroscopic collisions
  • Observation of ultracold atomic bubbles in orbital microgravity
  • Observation of Cooper pairs in a mesoscopic two-dimensional Fermi gas
  • Nanoscale imaging of phonon dynamics by electron microscopy
  • Electrically tunable quantum confinement of neutral excitons
  • Origin of structural degradation in Li-rich layered oxide cathode
  • Ni-electrocatalytic Csp3–Csp3 doubly decarboxylative coupling
  • Instantaneous tracking of earthquake growth with elastogravity signals
  • Lidar reveals pre-Hispanic low-density urbanism in the Bolivian Amazon
  • Island-specific evolution of a sex-primed autosome in a sexual planarian
  • The longitudinal dynamics and natural history of clonal haematopoiesis
  • Clonal dynamics of haematopoiesis across the human lifespan
  • Revival of light signalling in the postmortem mouse and human retina
  • ABO genotype alters the gut microbiota by regulating GalNAc levels in pigs
  • Prolonged viral suppression with anti-HIV-1 antibody therapy
  • Combination anti-HIV antibodies provide sustained virological suppression
  • Defining mitochondrial protein functions through deep multiomic profiling
  • Neoantigen quality predicts immunoediting in survivors of pancreatic cancer
  • Stromal changes in the aged lung induce an emergence from melanoma dormancy
  • Differential cofactor dependencies define distinct types of human enhancers
  • Discovery of non-squalene triterpenes
  • Limitations of gamete sequencing for crossover analysis
  • Retraction Note: A constraint on historic growth in global photosynthesis due to increasing CO2
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: The Atlantic Magazine: June 2022
